title: Power Automate - Microsoft Graph Connector Patterns And Constraints
applies_to: Power Automate Premium flows calling Microsoft Graph via built-in and custom connectors

confirmed_facts.fact: The 'HTTP with Microsoft Entra ID' connector (webcontentsv2) is a Premium Power Automate connector that acts on behalf of a signed-in user via delegated OAuth — it does NOT support app-only or service-principal Graph access natively.
HTTP with Microsoft Entra ID connector throttling limit: 100 API calls per connection per 60 seconds.
The older 'HTTP with Microsoft Entra ID (preauthorized)' connector (webcontents) is also delegated, on-behalf-of-user access for Microsoft services including Microsoft Graph.

Power Automate custom connector OAuth 2.0 configuration uses the authorization code method via APIHub; client credentials (app-only) flow is not a natively documented pattern for custom connectors in Power Automate.
Microsoft publishes an official tutorial for building a Power Automate custom connector that calls Microsoft Graph using Microsoft Entra ID OAuth 2.0 delegated permissions.

Power Automate supports flows owned and run by service principal application users, but this governs flow ownership and runtime identity within Power Platform — it does not enable app-only Graph calls through the native HTTP with Microsoft Entra ID connector.
Microsoft Graph supports JSON batching with up to 20 requests per batch, which can reduce connector round-trips in Power Automate flows that need to query multiple simulations in parallel.

implementation_notes: Use the delegated HTTP with Microsoft Entra ID connector as the primary no-code path for Graph calls in Power Automate; budget for a user-cached connection per environment.
For high-volume flows approaching the 100 calls/60s throttling limit, consider Graph JSON batching (up to 20 requests per batch) to reduce connector round-trips.

If strict non-user authentication is required, use an external intermediary (Azure Function, Logic App with Managed Identity, or script service) rather than expecting app-only calls from native Power Automate connectors.

Both the built-in connector and a custom Graph connector require the user to grant OAuth consent and create a connection; admin consent for the app registration does not substitute for the per-user connection creation step in Power Automate.
caveats: The native Power Automate connector model for Graph is delegated OAuth — any implementation guide claiming 'service account' or 'app-only' behavior through these connectors is inaccurate without an external intermediary.
Premium licensing is required for the HTTP with Microsoft Entra ID connector and custom connectors — this must be called out as a prerequisite before documenting connector-based flows.
